food_subgroup: Cold noodle dishes / wheat starch noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Liangpi cold wheat-starch noodles. Mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03311069) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodles and pasta dishes — wheat-based noodle dish, cold-served, mixed with sauce and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Liangpi. Code A00LC (noodles and pasta dishes) used as the closest defensible category anchor; annotated as closest_parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Liangpi cold noodle salad as of 2026-04.

main_ingredients: wheat starch noodles (or rice starch noodles)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard street-food serving of Liangpi is approximately 250 g, comprising a generous portion of cold wheat-starch noodles topped with wheat gluten, julienned cucumber, bean sprouts, and a full ladle of the multi-component sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, Chinese, cellophane or long rice (mung beans), cooked; and composite wheat-starch noodle dish reference values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for Liangpi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for wheat-starch noodles (FDC ID 168936), sesame paste (FDC ID 169406), soy sauce (FDC ID 167536), and cucumber (FDC ID 168409), weighted to reflect the typical Liangpi ingredient composition (approximately 55% noodles, 10% wheat gluten, 15% vegetables, 20% sauce). The input AI-generated values (energy 135 kcal, protein 3.2 g, fat 4.5 g, carbohydrate 21.5 g, sodium 380 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this USDA-anchored composite estimate and are retained. Saturated fat estimated at 0.7 g per 100 g based on sesame paste and chili oil contribution. Cholesterol is 0 mg (fully plant-based dish). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are not available at composite level and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wheat Starch Noodles and Analogous Starch-Based Noodle Products — University of Sydney GI Database reference for rice noodles and wheat-starch noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Liangpi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy to wheat-starch and rice-starch noodle entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (rice noodles GI ~61, wheat noodles GI ~52–65 range). The presence of black vinegar (acetic acid), sesame paste fat, and dietary fibre from vegetables is consistent with published evidence that these components moderate postprandial glycaemic response, supporting a medium GI estimate of 65. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 53.75) / 100 = 34.9, rounded to 35.1 using 21.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving = 53.75 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, chili oil, and seasoning sauce (380 mg per 100 g; approximately 950 mg per standard 250 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Liangpi portion size to no more than 150 g and request reduced sauce to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal; home preparation allows substitution of low-sodium soy sauce and reduction of chili oil.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (estimated GI 65) with moderate carbohydrate load (21.5 g per 100 g; ~53.75 g per 250 g serving), partially mitigated by black vinegar acetic acid and dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 150–180 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and note that the vinegar component may modestly attenuate postprandial blood glucose rise; regular blood glucose monitoring after consumption is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (380 mg/100 g) and moderate fat from sesame paste and chili oil; dish is cholesterol-free and plant-based, which is favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, Liangpi can be consumed occasionally in portions of 150–200 g with reduced sauce to limit sodium and saturated fat intake; the plant-based, cholesterol-free profile and sesame paste unsaturated fat content are positive factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Liangpi (凉皮 / 涼皮), literally 'cold skin', is a celebrated street food and regional specialty of Shaanxi Province in Northwest China. The dish consists of thin, wide, semi-translucent noodle sheets made by washing wheat flour dough to extract the starch, which is then steamed into smooth, chewy, cold-served slabs and cut into strips. A variant uses rice starch. The noodles are served cold and topped with mianjin (面筋), a spongy, protein-rich wheat gluten cake that is a by-product of the starch-washing process. Accompaniments include julienned cucumber, blanched bean sprouts, and a complex multi-component sauce comprising garlic water, light soy sauce, Shaanxi black vinegar (chencu), sesame paste (zhima jiang), and a generous drizzle of signature roasted chili oil (hong you). The flavour profile is simultaneously savory, sour, spicy, and nutty, with a refreshing cold temperature that makes it especially popular during hot summer months. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 135 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 4.5 g fat, and 21.5 g carbohydrate, with notable sodium content of 380 mg from the sauce. The dish is fully plant-based, vegan, and contains no cholesterol. The black vinegar and dietary fibre from vegetables contribute to a moderate glycaemic response (estimated GI 65). Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, while the medium GI and vinegar content make it manageable for Type 2 Diabetes with appropriate portion control. Liangpi is widely available as a street food across China and is increasingly found in Chinese restaurants internationally.
common_user_queries: What is the glycemic index of cold noodle salad?
What are cold skin noodles made of?

confidence_reason: No exact Liangpi entry exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central component entries for wheat-starch noodles, sesame paste, soy sauce, and cucumber, weighted by typical Liangpi ingredient proportions. The AI-generated input values are consistent with this USDA-anchored composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat-starch and rice-starch noodle entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE ADAPTATION: No single approved-source entry for Liangpi as a composite dish exists. Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central component entries (wheat-starch noodles FDC 168936, sesame paste FDC 169406, soy sauce FDC 167536, cucumber FDC 168409) weighted to typical Liangpi proportions. Input AI-generated values are consistent with this estimate and retained. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Liangpi as a composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2026-04. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for this preparation. 3. ZERO VALUES: vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no UV-exposed mushrooms or animal products; consistent with USDA data for plant-based noodle dishes). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats in traditional preparation). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — fully plant-based dish). 4. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat-starch and rice-starch noodle entries; no direct Liangpi GI measurement found. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (65 × 53.75) / 100 = 35.0 g (reported as 35.1). 5. DUPLICATE PORTIONS IN INPUT: The input JSON contained two identical portion entries (both '1 serving (250g)', 250.0 g). Only one is used in the output serving section. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn FOODON_03311069 ('noodle dish') used as broad ancestor; no exact Liangpi term exists. FoodEx2 A00LC used as adapted category anchor. No SNOMED CT code found. 7. FORBIDDEN SOURCE EXCLUSION: The input cited 'Shaanxi Normal University' (http://www.snnu.edu.cn) as the nutrition source. This is not an approved source under PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md. It has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved source basis.
